post.title: The Shape of a Runtime Contract
post.intent_statement: Keep a type honest across the async boundary where it is easiest to lose.
post.central_question: What must a runtime slot guarantee to be trusted?
post.core_insight: A contract you cannot see is a contract you cannot keep.
post.body_html:
Most of a system's character is decided at its edges — the contracts where one layer hands work to another. This week I sketched the runtime slot the ingestion path leans on.

The frame to hold

A slot is just a typed box with an id. The discipline is keeping the type honest across the async boundary, where it is easiest to quietly lose.

# The frame to hold: modern async + typing contract boundary
import asyncio
from typing import TypeAlias, Generic, TypeVar

T = TypeVar("T")
PayloadID: TypeAlias = str

class RuntimeSlot(Generic[T]):
    def __init__(self, slot_id: PayloadID, data: T) -> None:
        self.slot_id = slot_id
        self.data = data

async def execute(path: RuntimeSlot[bytes]) -> None:
    # 3.12+ event-loop execution path; never block the caller
    await asyncio.sleep(0)

What the boundary guarantees

  • A slot's slot_id is opaque — never parsed, only compared.
  • Ingestion owns creation; the runtime only reads.
  • Failure is a value, not an exception, once we cross into the loop.
A contract you cannot see is a contract you cannot keep.

Order of operations

  1. Validate the payload at the door.
  2. Wrap it in a slot with a fresh id.
  3. Hand the slot to the loop and forget the raw bytes.

The client mirrors it with one narrow call:

const slot = await runtime.admit({ id: makeId(), data: payload });
if (!slot.ok) return fail(slot.error);

More on the ingestion side in the field notes. The shape is small; holding it honestly is the work.
